title: Capstone: File Room Handoff
summary: Use the terminal to sort a messy event folder, protect important notes, remove temporary clutter, and mark the workspace ready.

sketchBlocks.title: Read the workspace before you change it
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A good handoff starts with inspection. Before moving anything, use `pwd` to confirm where you are and `ls` to see what is already in the workspace.

Example:
```bash
pwd
ls
```

Line by line:
- First, `pwd` prints your current location so you know you are working in the lesson workspace.
- Second, `ls` lists the visible files and folders in that location.
- If you need more detail, you can move into a folder with `cd` and run `ls` again.

In this capstone, that habit helps you avoid putting files in the wrong place or deleting the wrong item.

sketchBlocks.title: Build a clear folder structure
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Once you know what is in the workspace, create a structure that makes sense for the next person.

Example:
```bash
mkdir -p handoff/notes handoff/archive
```

Step by step:
- `mkdir -p` creates folders.
- `handoff/notes` makes a notes folder inside `handoff`.
- `handoff/archive` makes an archive folder in the same command.
- The `-p` option lets the parent folder be created as needed.

This is useful in a handoff because grouped folders make it obvious where active notes and saved backups belong.

sketchBlocks.title: Move, copy, and rename with purpose
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Organizing a workspace usually means deciding which files should be moved, which should be backed up, and which should get clearer names.

Example:
```bash
mv draft.txt handoff/notes/event-notes.txt
cp handoff/notes/event-notes.txt handoff/archive/event-notes-backup.txt
```

Line by line:
- First, `mv` relocates `draft.txt` and gives it a better name at the same time.
- Second, `cp` makes a second copy in the archive folder.
- After these commands, one file is the working note and the other is the backup.

That pattern is common in real work: keep one clean main file and one safety copy.

sketchBlocks.title: Remove clutter carefully
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
A clean handoff should not include temporary clutter. Remove only the file you truly mean to discard.

Example:
```bash
rm temp-list.txt
ls
```

Step by step:
- `rm temp-list.txt` deletes that one temporary file.
- `ls` checks what remains after the removal.
- Running a check command right after a destructive command is a safe habit.

In this project, you will remove a temporary item while keeping the important notes and backup intact.

sketchBlocks.title: Verify the final handoff state
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The last step is proof. A handoff is not finished until you confirm the final structure and leave a clear marker.

Example:
```bash
cat handoff/ready.txt
ls handoff
```

Line by line:
- First, `cat handoff/ready.txt` shows the marker file contents so you can confirm it exists.
- Second, `ls handoff` lists the top-level handoff folder contents.
- Together, these checks show both the marker and the organized folders.

Verification matters because the next person should be able to open the workspace and trust that it is ready.

quizDraft.title: Create the park survey request folder
quizDraft.prompt: You are helping a park survey team prepare a terminal map. Create final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests and add final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests/north-trail.txt so the team has a concrete request log to hand off.
quizDraft.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

help.concept: This support text was repaired because the original wording revealed the answer too directly.
help.hint_1: Use the terminal to make one small workspace change at a time, then inspect the result in the file explorer.
help.hint_2: Use the role or evidence named in the prompt instead of relying on answer wording.

quizDraft.starterCode: # Use the terminal for this Linux project task.
quizDraft.solutionCode:
mkdir -p final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests
printf 'North trail request log\n' > final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests/north-trail.txt
ls final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey
quizDraft.instructions: You are helping a park survey team prepare a terminal map. Create final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests and add final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/requests/north-trail.txt so the team has a concrete request log to hand off.

quizDraft.title: Add the map notes folder
quizDraft.prompt: Continue the same park survey workspace. Keep the request log from step 1, then add final-capstone-file-room-handoff-park-survey/maps/current-location.txt as the team's navigation note deliverable.
quizDraft.hint: Read the Linux task "Add the map notes folder" and identify the folders or files you must create or organize.
